author: serenita_io
content:
The rest of us is saying it's a bad thing to default to (for the reasons mentioned earlier) that ultimately hurts the unsophisticated proposer.

I'm going to say again that I believe the best thing we can do is make those headers required, giving the builders information on how quickly they need to return a response. We just need to agree on a good default value, whether that is 300ms into the slot or 1 second. I don't think 0 is a good default.

I understand your desire to let the auction "play out" on the proposer side. However, since we all like to talk about rational behavior, that is not the rational thing to do for proposer since it only leads to lower bids if your goal is to propose by some time into the slot.

author: potuz
content: Zero was never a proposal and yes that info can go in the header. I am **only** asking to agree on a short default

author: serenita_io
content:
Ok, if you agree on the principle and requiring those headers, then you're right, the only thing we need to agree on is how far into the slot validators should aim to propose.

Today, most validators propose about 1-1.2s into the slot. Post-Gloas we’ll have a shorter attestation deadline but also less to do before the deadline (beacon block is smaller to propagate and no need to process the EL payload). Given that, aiming to propose 1s into the slot still seems pretty reasonable. I'd personally be ok with defaulting in the 0.5-1s range.

author: potuz
content: No how far into the slot the request goes is zero by default and we should not change this. The question is on the deadline for the request itself

author: serenita_io
content: I think we're talking about the same thing at this point. I never said we should make the request later into the slot, I meant that validators should make the request at t=0 and *aim* to propose (=publish an actual beacon block) 500-1000ms into the slot.

author: potuz
content: Ok we're getting into the same page, only difference is that I want to move the 500-1000ms range into one that has 300ms as max by default. Proposers in Adelaide that want to risk it can increase that default

author: potuz
content: No need to be stressing every block for them

author: serenita_io
content:
Ok, in my opinion 2s *should be* enough time to distribute a smaller post-Gloas beacon block across the globe, without attesters having to verify the execution payload.
But I am very open to hearing why that would not be enough.

author: serenita_io
content: And I will honestly say my numbers here are only roughly based on what I'm observing currently on mainnet + a bit of intuition on how Gloas changes things. It would be great if we could make a data-driven decision on this.
